subcategory: Less-optimistic scenario: budgetary requirement increase (govt bears oil industry costs)
notes: Under the less-optimistic assumption that government continues bearing the oil industry's costs, budgetary requirements increase by about $25 million.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 256: Same memorandum, Appendix I ("Iran's yearly budgetary deficit"), Washington, undated, PDF p. 687 (printed p. 650)

subcategory: Less-optimistic scenario: aggregate requirement
notes: Aggregate requirement under the less-optimistic scenario stated as $70 million ($45 million plus $25 million for oil-industry-loss expenses, less $12 million of increased customs revenue, per the document's own bracketed arithmetic).
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 256: Same memorandum, Appendix I ("Iran's yearly budgetary deficit"), Washington, undated, PDF p. 687 (printed p. 650)

subcategory: Less-optimistic scenario at improved 60 rials/USD rate
notes: If a rate of 60 rials to the dollar (instead of 50) could be maintained when aid is disbursed, the $70 million estimate would reduce to about $58 million.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 256: Same memorandum, Appendix I ("Iran's yearly budgetary deficit"), Washington, undated, PDF p. 687 (printed p. 650)

subcategory: Ministry of War budget allocation
notes: "Last year's" (1952) Iranian budget allocation for the Ministry of War, part of the security-forces total below.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 256: Same memorandum, Appendix I ("Iran's yearly budgetary deficit"), Washington, undated, PDF p. 687 (printed p. 650)

subcategory: Gendarmerie budget allocation
notes: "Last year's" (1952) Iranian budget allocation for the Gendarmerie.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 256: Same memorandum, Appendix I ("Iran's yearly budgetary deficit"), Washington, undated, PDF p. 687 (printed p. 650)

subcategory: Police Department budget allocation
notes: "Last year's" (1952) Iranian budget allocation for the Police Department.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 256: Same memorandum, Appendix I ("Iran's yearly budgetary deficit"), Washington, undated, PDF p. 687 (printed p. 650)

subcategory: Combined security-forces budget total
notes: Ministry of War + Gendarmerie + Police Department combined: 3,271,250,000 rials, equivalent to $67 million -- the sum a covert dollar-to-rial conversion program was designed to help cover.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 256: Same memorandum, Appendix I ("Iran's yearly budgetary deficit"), Washington, undated, PDF p. 687 (printed p. 650)

subcategory: Average import exchange rate before oil-revenue curtailment
notes: Footnote: the average import exchange rate before curtailment of oil revenues (i.e. before mid-1951) was 44 rials per dollar.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 256: Same memorandum, Appendix I ("Iran's yearly budgetary deficit"), Washington, undated, PDF p. 687 (printed p. 650)

subcategory: Present open-market import rate (as of this document)
notes: Footnote: the present open market import rate is about 90 rials per dollar, described as "highly artificial" with little real demand at that rate.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 256: Same memorandum, Appendix I ("Iran's yearly budgetary deficit"), Washington, undated, PDF p. 687 (printed p. 650)

subcategory: Gold and credits owed to Iran by the USSR
notes: Return of $21 million in gold and credits owed to Iran by the USSR would provide only temporary relief; Iran was already using at least half that amount as currency note cover.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 259: Draft National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, August 12, 1953, PDF p. 696 (printed p. 659)

subcategory: Discount offered by Iran to attract oil buyers
notes: Despite Iran's willingness to extend a 50 percent discount off world prices (and easing tanker supply), sales remained minimal because major companies avoided clashing with AIOC.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 259: Draft National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, August 12, 1953, PDF p. 696 (printed p. 659)

subcategory: US emergency economic assistance grant to Zahedi government
notes: The White House announced on September 5, 1953 that $45 million would be made available for immediate economic assistance to Iran, in addition to existing military and technical assistance programs -- the single most-cited aid figure across the post-coup documents in this volume.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 301: Letter From Iranian Prime Minister Zahedi to President Eisenhower (editorial footnote reporting the White House announcement), Tehran, August 26, 1953 (announcement: Washington, September 5, 1953), PDF p. 752 (printed p. 715)

subcategory: Zahedi's view that emergency grant is insufficient
notes: Prime Minister Zahedi considered the $45,000,000 grant too small to undertake necessary development and job-creating projects and considered resigning before being reassured by Ambassador Henderson.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 319: Briefing Notes Prepared in the Central Intelligence Agency for Director of Central Intelligence Dulles, Washington, undated (NSC Briefing, ca. September 17, 1953), PDF p. 797 (printed p. 760)

subcategory: Point IV director Warne's estimate of aid needed to cover 7-month budget deficit
notes: Point IV Director Warne estimated $37,000,000 of American aid was required to meet Iran's budget deficit for the next 7 months.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 319: Briefing Notes Prepared in the Central Intelligence Agency for Director of Central Intelligence Dulles, Washington, undated (NSC Briefing, ca. September 17, 1953), PDF p. 797 (printed p. 760)

subcategory: Of which, remaining for economic development
notes: Of the $37,000,000 estimate, $8,000,000 would remain available for economic development after covering the 7-month deficit.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 319: Briefing Notes Prepared in the Central Intelligence Agency for Director of Central Intelligence Dulles, Washington, undated (NSC Briefing, ca. September 17, 1953), PDF p. 797 (printed p. 760)

subcategory: US emergency aid received by Zahedi government (confirmed)
notes: CIA monthly report confirms the new Iranian government received emergency aid in the amount of $45 million from the U.S.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 326: Monthly Report Prepared in Directorate of Plans, CIA -- "Iran, September 1953", Washington, undated (October 1953 monthly report), PDF p. 815 (printed p. 778)

subcategory: Remaining runway on emergency aid program (as of late Oct 1953)
notes: Ambassador stated the emergency aid program now under way had five more months to run; at the end of that time Iran must find some other source of revenue.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 341: Despatch From the Embassy in Iran to the Department of State, Tehran, October 30, 1953, PDF p. 856 (printed p. 819)

subcategory: Emergency $45m grant projected exhaustion
notes: The $45 million emergency grant extended soon after Zahedi took office was projected to cover operating expenses until about February or March 1954.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Abadan refinery restoration cost estimate
notes: An expenditure variously estimated from $30,000,000 to $60,000,000 would be required to place the Abadan refinery back into partial operation after its post-1951 disrepair.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Abadan refinery historical throughput
notes: The Abadan refinery, the world's largest, formerly had a through-put in excess of 500,000 barrels per day before the 1951 shutdown.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Abadan refinery reduced target throughput (restart plan)
notes: Even a reduced through-put target of 300,000 barrels per day would present a severe marketing/distribution problem given the post-war shift toward refining near consumption points.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Iranian crude production reached prior to 1951 shutdown
notes: Iranian crude production reached about 650,000 barrels per day prior to the 1951 shutdown.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Middle East share of Eastern Hemisphere oil refining
notes: In 1945, 82 percent of all oil refined in the Eastern Hemisphere was processed in the Middle East.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Middle East share of Eastern Hemisphere oil refining (1953)
notes: By 1953 the Middle East's share of Eastern Hemisphere refining had declined to 19 percent, as refining relocated toward consumption points.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: European share of Eastern Hemisphere oil refining
notes: In 1945, European refineries processed only 6 percent of Eastern Hemisphere refining volume.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: European share of Eastern Hemisphere oil refining (1953)
notes: By 1953 Europe's share had risen to 63 percent of Eastern Hemisphere refining volume.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Middle East net oil income per barrel (royalties + taxes)
notes: Existing net income (royalties and taxes) received by Middle Eastern oil-producing countries was approximately 70 to 80 cents per barrel.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Saudi Arabia projected 1953 net oil income
notes: Saudi Arabia was projected to receive net income in excess of $200 million for the year 1953 -- offered as a comparator for what Iran might obtain from a settlement.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Oil revenue share of government foreign-exchange income (pre-nationalization)
notes: In the period immediately prior to nationalization, oil operations provided over half of the government's total foreign exchange income.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Oil revenue share of government foreign-exchange income (two-thirds figure)
notes: Elsewhere in the same NIE, Iran is described as receiving 'about two-thirds' of its total foreign exchange revenue through oil operations immediately prior to nationalization -- kept as a separate row since it differs from the 'over half' figure stated elsewhere in the same document (not reconciled, both preserved).
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Oil revenue share of total government revenue (pre-nationalization)
notes: The government obtained 'very close to one-half' of its total revenue, directly or indirectly, from oil operations before nationalization.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Foreign-exchange reserves drawn down in first post-nationalization year
notes: In the first year after nationalization, the government made up lost oil revenue primarily by selling foreign exchange, drawing down $62 million of reserves.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: IMF drawing in first post-nationalization year
notes: The government also obtained $8.75 million from the International Monetary Fund during the first post-nationalization year.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Remaining gold/FX reserves required as currency cover
notes: By mid-1952 the remaining gold and foreign exchange reserves of approximately $180 million had to be retained as legal cover for the currency, forcing Mosadeq to print additional rial notes beyond the statutory limit.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Import cutback once reliant on note-printing to cover deficit
notes: When the government turned to issuing additional rial notes (after exhausting reserves), imports had to be cut 40 to 50 percent, driving serious inflation.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Current budgetary deficit rate (per latest data, Nov 1953)
notes: Latest data indicated the government was incurring a budgetary deficit of about 400 million rials per month.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Annualized deficit including year-end bonuses
notes: Allowing for customary year-end bonus payments, the monthly rate amounts to a deficit of about 5 billion rials per year.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Rial/USD rate used for the Nov 1953 deficit estimate
notes: At the recently established rate of 90 rials to the dollar, the 5 billion rial/year deficit is equivalent to about $55 million.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Annualized deficit in USD (at 90 rials/USD)
notes: Dollar equivalent of the ~5 billion rial/year deficit at the 90 rials/USD rate.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Zahedi government's committed development program
notes: The Zahedi Government committed to a development program calling for annual expenditure of 3.9 billion rials ($43 million) to raise living standards and reduce unemployment.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Continuation of technical/economic aid program, annual rate

citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Estimated required emergency economic assistance rate
notes: Emergency economic assistance at a rate of at least $50 to $55 million a year, alongside continued $23 million technical/economic aid, judged necessary to meet minimum budgetary and FX requirements.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Possible additional margin needed in current US fiscal year

citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 347: National Intelligence Estimate, Washington, November 16, 1953, PDF p. 878 (printed p. 841)

subcategory: Near East/Africa regional contingency fund (FY1955 budget, not Iran-specific)

subcategory: Projected Middle East share of total US peacetime oil requirement by 1975

subcategory: Cumulative US emergency grants to Iran (updated total, March 1954)
notes: By March 1954 this memo describes Iran's treasury as restored 'with the aid of $51 million in US emergency grants' -- a cumulative total above the original $45 million September 1953 tranche, implying an additional ~$6 million had been extended by this point (not itself separately itemized in the source).
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 364: Memorandum From the CIA Representative on the NSC 5402 Working Group (Waller) to the Special Assistant to the DCI (Jackson), Washington, March 19, 1954, PDF p. 953 (printed p. 916)

subcategory: Emergency aid exhaustion date (revised, March 1954 estimate)
notes: Same memo: present US emergency aid was expected to be exhausted in June 1954, the deadline pressuring an oil settlement.
citation: FRUS 1952-1954, Iran, 1951-1954, Document 364: Memorandum From the CIA Representative on the NSC 5402 Working Group (Waller) to the Special Assistant to the DCI (Jackson), Washington, March 19, 1954, PDF p. 953 (printed p. 916)
